metadata.summary: Multi-level caching (CDN → app in-memory → Redis/Valkey → DB) with explicit pattern selection and mandatory per-key TTL, invalidation trigger, and hit-rate monitoring.

content.title: Caching Strategy
content.summary: Multi-level caching (CDN → app in-memory → Redis/Valkey → DB) with explicit pattern selection (cache-aside, write-through, write-behind, SWR) and mandatory per-key TTL, invalidation trigger, and hit-rate monitoring. Every cache layer requires a documented invalidation path and stampede protection for endpoints serving > 100 RPS.

why.p: Unbounded read traffic can saturate databases even when queries are well-tuned. Caching reduces latency and DB load, but only when hit rates are measured and staleness tolerance is explicitly decided. Without stampede protection (singleflight / SWR) a cache miss under load sends all concurrent requests to the DB simultaneously, reproducing the original problem.

list.item: Read-heavy endpoints with bounded staleness tolerance (product catalog, user profile, settings).
Expensive computations whose inputs repeat (LLM responses, image transforms, search aggregations).
Static or near-static API responses behind a CDN or edge cache.
Session storage, OAuth token cache, rate-limit counters — natural Redis fit.
Aggregation/dashboard responses where 30-60 s freshness is acceptable.
Reducing DB load when query plans are tuned but still slow under sustained traffic.
Idempotency: cache request hashes to deduplicate retries.
Highly volatile data (live counts, current bid prices) where any staleness is unacceptable.
Per-user data with no reuse pattern (expected hit ratio less than 50% — wasted memory).
Small datasets that fit in app memory already (use a plain dict).
Without a measurement plan — caching without hit-rate monitoring is dead weight.
When cache invalidation logic is more complex than the underlying query.

content.summary: Cache-aside (lazy loading), write-through, and write-behind patterns with rules for pattern selection and Redis key naming.

rule.statement: Set a TTL on every Redis key without exception. A key without TTL makes Redis an append-only memory store and causes unbounded growth.
rule.rationale: Redis does not garbage-collect keys without TTL. Even "permanent" configuration data should have a long TTL (e.g., 86400s) so stale entries are eventually cleaned.

rule.statement: Use the namespace convention {service}:{entity}:{id}:{version} for all cache keys. Enforce this in code review and via a linting rule on string literals.
rule.rationale: Collisions across services or versions produce silent staleness bugs. Namespacing prevents them and makes KEYS scans interpretable.

rule.statement: Before adding any cache layer, measure the baseline hit ratio target and instrument cache_hit_total, cache_miss_total, and cache_evictions in Prometheus/Grafana. Do not add caching without monitoring.
rule.rationale: Caching without hit-rate monitoring is dead weight. Low hit rates waste memory with no latency benefit; high eviction rates indicate mismatched TTL or maxmemory settings.

section.name: Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
section.p: Read: check cache first; on miss, load from DB, write to cache, return. Write: update DB, invalidate or update cache key. Best for read-heavy data with bounded staleness tolerance.

rule.statement: For cache-aside on endpoints serving > 100 RPS, implement stampede protection (singleflight / stale-while-revalidate). A bare cache-aside miss under load sends N concurrent requests to the DB simultaneously.
rule.rationale: On miss, all concurrent waiters bypass the cache together. Singleflight coalesces them: one fetches, the rest wait for the result.

section.reference: Cache-aside decorator with Redis, TTL, and invalidation helper
Async singleflight with Redis NX mutex for stampede protection

section.name: Write-Through
section.p: Write to DB first, then update cache atomically. Reads always hit cache (populated on first write). Best for data that is written and immediately re-read (user profiles, settings).

rule.statement: In write-through, always write to the DB before writing to the cache. Reverse order creates a window where the cache shows new data but the DB still holds stale data.
rule.rationale: If the DB write fails after a successful cache update, the cache is poisoned until TTL expires. Writing DB first means a failed cache write is safe — the next read will miss and re-populate.

section.name: Write-Behind (Write-Back)
section.p: Write to cache immediately, queue DB write for async flush. Reduces write latency; risks data loss if cache crashes before flush. Use only for high-write counters and analytics.

rule.statement: Only use write-behind for data where transient loss is acceptable (counters, analytics). Never use for financial, authentication, or order data.
rule.rationale: Write-behind defers persistence. Cache crash before flush = data loss. For correctness-critical data, write-through or write-through with outbox is required.

content.summary: TTL-based, event-based, and version-based invalidation strategies; HTTP ETag and Cache-Control directives for CDN/browser caching.

rule.statement: Document the invalidation trigger for every cache key explicitly in code (comment or config entry). TTL alone is not sufficient for correctness-critical data.
rule.rationale: TTL-only caches serve stale data for the full TTL window after a write. Correctness-critical data (inventory, pricing) requires event-based or version-based invalidation triggered by writes.

section.name: Event-Based Invalidation
section.p: Invalidate specific keys on write events. Use tag-based grouping (Redis SADD cache:tag:T key; on invalidate: SMEMBERS + DELETE) to invalidate related keys atomically without KEYS * scans.

rule.statement: Never use KEYS * or SCAN with a wildcard pattern on production Redis for cache invalidation. Use tag sets instead.
rule.rationale: KEYS * is O(N) and blocks the Redis event loop. Tag-based invalidation is O(members) and non-blocking.

section.name: Version-Based Invalidation
section.p: Bump a version counter (INCR cache:version:entity) on writes. Cache keys include the version: {entity}:v{version}:{id}. Old versions are never explicitly deleted — they expire via TTL.
Best for bulk invalidation (invalidate all products) without KEYS scans.

rule.statement: Use Cache-Control: public, max-age=N, stale-while-revalidate=M for public, slowly-changing API responses. Use Cache-Control: private, max-age=0, must-revalidate for per-user data. Never omit Cache-Control on API endpoints — browser and CDN defaults are inconsistent.
rule.rationale: Missing Cache-Control allows CDNs and browsers to use their own heuristics, which may cache private data publicly or cache public data too aggressively.

rule.statement: Cache misses (404 responses) with a short TTL (60s) to prevent DB hammering on bad inputs. Never cache nulls permanently.
rule.rationale: Without negative caching, repeated requests for non-existent resources hit the DB every time. Permanent null caching causes an app to believe deleted entities never existed.

content.summary: Failure modes: stampede, stale data, memory pressure, hot key, pickle RCE, async/sync client mix-up, write order inversion.

antipattern.description: Cache stampede (thundering herd): N concurrent misses all hit the DB simultaneously.
antipattern.reason: Bare cache-aside with no stampede protection sends every concurrent waiter to the DB on miss. Under load, this can produce N × single-request DB load, exactly reproducing the original problem.

antipattern.description: Using pickle to serialize cache values in Redis.
antipattern.reason: Pickle deserializes arbitrary Python objects. If cache contents are attacker-controlled, deserialization is remote code execution. Use JSON or msgpack with schema validation.

antipattern.description: Importing redis.Redis (sync) in async FastAPI code.
antipattern.reason: Sync Redis calls block the event loop, serializing all concurrent requests. Import redis.asyncio.Redis for async services.

antipattern.description: Agent sets TTL=86400 by default without computing it from staleness tolerance.
antipattern.reason: 24-hour TTL is too long for most user-visible data. Compute TTL from the agreed staleness tolerance (e.g., 1800s for user profile if 30min stale is acceptable). Stale data for 24h produces customer complaints.

antipattern.description: Cache key includes mutable or high-cardinality arguments (timestamps, request IDs).
antipattern.reason: A key that encodes request_id or current timestamp generates a new key per request, producing 0% hit rate and unbounded key growth in Redis.

antipattern.description: Hot key: a single Redis key receiving all traffic (e.g., global feature flag served to every request).
antipattern.reason: A single Redis key is bound to one shard. At extreme traffic, it saturates network bandwidth or CPU for that shard. Mitigate with local in-process cache with short TTL + Redis fallback.

antipattern.description: Adding a cache layer without Prometheus metrics in the same PR.
antipattern.reason: Cache effectiveness is invisible without hit-rate monitoring. An unmonitored cache may have 10% hit rate (wasted infrastructure) or cause subtle staleness bugs that are only visible via metrics divergence.

template.purpose: Cache-aside decorator with Redis, TTL, key builder, and invalidation helper

script.purpose: Async cache-aside with thundering-herd protection via Redis NX mutex
